skill.name: httpx
skill.description: Guides Python developers in using HTTPX for synchronous and asynchronous HTTP clients, streaming, authentication, timeouts, and service integrations.
skill.summary: Use HTTPX patterns for modern Python HTTP clients with sync, async, streaming, and authentication support.
skill.author: bossjones

security_audit.summary: AI review found no malicious behavior or prompt injection in the scanned files. The critical and high static findings are false positives caused by markdown code fences, HTTP protocol wording, placeholder URLs, and authentication examples. The skill is safe to publish with a low risk note because it teaches network requests, credential headers, and file downloads.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged markdown triple backticks as Ruby or shell backtick execution. These lines delimit documentation examples and do not execute commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each cited line is a markdown code fence around Python, text, or bash examples. No executable skill code invokes a shell command.

low_findings.title: Placeholder HTTP URLs in Documentation
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged hardcoded URLs. The URLs are examples for HTTPX usage, mostly reserved example domains or documentation prompts, not hidden exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in HTTP client examples and prior evaluation text. No code in the skill automatically contacts these endpoints.

low_findings.title: Credential Terms Are Example Parameters
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged API key and bearer token terms. The skill uses placeholder parameters and headers to teach authentication and does not read environment variables or collect secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited code uses function parameters or placeholder strings for authentication examples. I found no os.environ, process.env, credential storage, or network exfiltration logic.

low_findings.title: File Writes Are Download Examples
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged Python file writes. These examples write streamed HTTP responses to local files, which is a normal download pattern but should be adapted carefully by users.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are visibly part of streaming download examples. They do not overwrite system paths or run automatically, but user-supplied filenames require normal care.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Is Protocol Wording
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged weak cryptography, but the cited content describes HTTPX, HTTP versions, and production features. No cryptographic algorithm implementation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static trigger appears tied to HTTP-related wording, not MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar weak algorithms. The file is documentation for a client library.

low_findings.title: Combined Risk Heuristic Is Unsupported by Context
low_findings.description: The critical heuristic combined network, credential, filesystem, and command signals. Context shows documentation examples and markdown syntax, not obfuscation or malicious automation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous combination depends on the command-execution false positive. I found no obfuscation, encoded payloads, or instructions to bypass security review.

content.user_title: Build Python HTTP Clients with HTTPX
content.value_statement: Python teams need reliable patterns for HTTP requests, streaming, and async APIs. This skill provides HTTPX examples for clients, authentication, timeouts, retries, and downloads.
actual_capabilities: Shows synchronous and asynchronous HTTPX request patterns.
Demonstrates Client and AsyncClient configuration with base URLs, headers, and timeouts.
Explains Basic, Bearer token, and custom authentication examples.
Provides streaming download, upload, and progress monitoring patterns.
Includes error handling, retry logic, WebSocket, and installation examples.
Lists optional packages for HTTP/2, WebSocket support, and progress bars.
limitations: It is documentation and does not install or run HTTPX by itself.
Examples use placeholder endpoints, tokens, usernames, and passwords.
WebSocket and progress examples require optional third-party packages.
Retry examples are basic and may need production policy controls.

use_cases.title: Create API clients
use_cases.description: Build reusable HTTPX clients with authentication, timeout handling, and shared connection configuration.

use_cases.title: Stream large responses
use_cases.description: Download or upload large API payloads without loading full files into memory.

use_cases.title: Modernize service integrations
use_cases.description: Move Python integrations toward async requests, HTTP/2 support, and safer request lifecycle management.

prompt_templates.title: Make a basic request
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to use HTTPX for a simple GET request with status checking and JSON response handling.

prompt_templates.title: Configure an API client
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a reusable HTTPX client pattern with a base URL, bearer token header, timeout settings, and clean shutdown.

prompt_templates.title: Handle large transfers
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an HTTPX streaming download flow that writes chunks to a chosen file and reports progress without high memory use.

prompt_templates.title: Build an async integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Build an async HTTPX workflow that runs concurrent requests, applies retry logic, handles errors, and closes resources correctly.

output_examples.input: Ask for a basic HTTPX GET request pattern.
output_examples.output: A short Python-oriented explanation that covers status checks, JSON parsing, timeout use, and error handling.

output_examples.input: Ask for a streaming download approach.
output_examples.output: A chunked download plan that avoids loading the full response into memory and tracks progress.

output_examples.input: Ask for an authenticated async client design.
output_examples.output: A reusable client structure with bearer authentication, AsyncClient lifecycle handling, and request methods.

best_practices: Set explicit timeouts for every client or request.
Reuse client instances so connection pooling works correctly.
Keep real credentials outside prompts and source files.
anti_patterns: Creating a new client for every single request.
Using placeholder tokens or example domains in production code.
Writing downloaded content to unvalidated user-supplied paths.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps create Python HTTPX clients for synchronous requests, asynchronous workflows, authentication, streaming, and error handling.

faq.question: Does it execute network requests automatically?
faq.answer: No. The skill contains documentation examples. Users decide when to run generated code in their own environment.

faq.question: Can it help with async Python APIs?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes AsyncClient examples for concurrent requests, streaming, and clean async resource shutdown.

faq.question: Does HTTPX support HTTP/2?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill shows optional installation guidance for HTTP/2 support with HTTPX extras.

faq.question: How should credentials be handled?
faq.answer: Use secure runtime configuration or secret managers. Do not paste real tokens into prompts or source files.

faq.question: Is WebSocket support built into HTTPX?
faq.answer: The skill points to httpx-ws for WebSocket support because that feature requires an additional package.
